neighborhoods_visited: Medina - Jemaa el-Fna and the souks
Medina - Mouassine and the northern riads
guide_description: Three days covering Marrakech's essential layers: the theatrical main square and its labyrinthine souks, the quieter northern medina with its mosques and palaces, and the French colonial ville nouvelle with its gardens and museum culture. Each day is geographically self-contained within walking distance of itself.

days.cluster_tagline: The living theater of the medina - snake charmers, spice mountains, and medieval market lanes
days.neighborhood_focus: Medina - Jemaa el-Fna and the souks
days.neighborhood_description: The southern medina anchored by Jemaa el-Fna, the great square that UNESCO recognized not as a building but as an intangible cultural space. North of the square the souks fan out into specialized lanes - dyers, tanners, saddlers, copper workers - following a medieval commercial geography that has barely shifted in five centuries.

morning.notes: The square reads entirely differently by time of day. Morning is the quietest window - juice stalls set up, a handful of storytellers warm up, and the Atlas Mountains are visible to the south before the heat haze builds. Avoid the orange juice carts closest to the central fountain; the stalls on the western edge give you juice at the same price with less aggressive touting. The square's UNESCO inscription is for oral heritage, not the architecture - it is protected as a living cultural practice.

afternoon.notes: Non-Muslims cannot enter the mosque but the surrounding gardens are open and give the best exterior view of the 70-meter minaret, which set the template for the Giralda in Seville and the Hassan Tower in Rabat. The garden path on the northern side, running parallel to Avenue Mohammed V, gives a clean sight line to the full minaret face without tour groups blocking the foreground.

evening.notes: Around an hour before sunset the square transforms - food stall grills ignite, Gnawa musicians arrive with their metal castanets and three-stringed guembri lutes, and the crowd density triples. Watch the transition from any of the rooftop cafe terraces on the northern edge of the square - Cafe de France and Cafe Glacier both provide this vantage without being notably better than each other. On the square itself, stay on the outer ring near the food stalls where movement is possible rather than in the central performer scrum.

if_time_permits.notes: The dyers' souk sits at the end of Rue Mouassine and is most active before noon when the dyed skeins of wool are hung to dry in primary colors across the narrow lane. It is a working quarter, not a museum exhibit - purchases are not required and the workers are accustomed to observers. Morning light drops directly into the lane between roughly 9am and 11am.

A restored 16th-century Islamic garden inside a historic riad in the Mouassine quarter - split into an exotic garden and an Islamic garden with a central irrigation channel. The tower at the rear gives one of the few legal rooftop views over the medina roofscape without having to buy a meal. Modest entry fee, rarely crowded before 10am.

days.cluster_name: The Northern Medina - Palaces and Madrasa
days.cluster_tagline: Zellij tilework, cedar ceilings, and Saadian architecture in the quieter upper medina
days.neighborhood_focus: Medina - Mouassine and the northern riads
days.neighborhood_description: The northern and central medina above the souks, where the Saadian and Marinid-era monuments are concentrated. The Ben Youssef Madrasa, the Musee de Marrakech, and the narrow lanes of the Mouassine quarter sit within a ten-minute walk of each other - and the crowds thin noticeably once you move north of Souk el-Kebir.

morning.notes: A 14th-century Quranic school rebuilt by the Saadians in 1565, capable of housing 900 students in the cells that ring the central courtyard. The courtyard itself is the architectural masterpiece - three registers of decoration stacked vertically, from intricate zellij mosaic at foot level through carved stucco arabesque in the middle to cedarwood muqarnas lattice at the top. Arrive before 9:30am to photograph the courtyard without tour group overflow from the central corridor.

afternoon.notes: Housed in the 19th-century Mnebhi Palace, the museum's collection of Moroccan arts and crafts is secondary to the architecture itself - in particular the central hammam dome converted into a grand chandelier hall, with a massive brass lamp suspended over the marble-flagged floor. The combined ticket with Ben Youssef Madrasa and the Koubba Ba'adiyn saves money and the three sites are adjacent. The Koubba, a lone surviving Almoravid pavilion from the 12th century, is the oldest standing monument in Marrakech and often skipped.

evening.notes: Built in the 1890s for Si Moussa and his son Ba Ahmed, grand viziers to the sultan - the name means 'brilliance'. The palace has 160 rooms arranged around eight courtyards, of which roughly a dozen are open. The grand riad courtyard with its painted cedar ceiling is the set piece, but the smaller harem courtyard to the south, usually less crowded, has better tilework at floor level. Exit via Rue Riad Zitoun el-Jdid and walk north back toward the medina rather than retracing your route.

if_time_permits.notes: The mausoleum of the Saadian dynasty, sealed by the Alaouite sultan Moulay Ismail in the 17th century and rediscovered only in 1917 - which is why the tilework and carved stucco are in remarkable condition relative to their age. The site is small and the entry corridor narrow; queues build after 10am. The Chamber of Twelve Columns is the interior to seek out, but do not miss the outdoor garden of lesser tombs behind it, which are more atmospheric and less visited.

days.cluster_tagline: French colonial modernism, olive groves, and the Yves Saint Laurent legacy
days.neighborhood_description: The French-planned new city built from 1913 outside the medina walls, with broad tree-lined boulevards, art deco facades, and a very different urban rhythm from the medieval labyrinth. The Menara and Majorelle gardens anchor the western and northern edges respectively, and the Musee Yves Saint Laurent sits adjacent to Majorelle.

morning.notes: Designed by French painter Jacques Majorelle from 1923 and later owned by Yves Saint Laurent, who restored it after buying it in 1980 to save it from hotel development. The signature cobalt blue - Majorelle Blue, a color he patented - covers the studio building and pots against which the succulent collection is arranged. Book timed-entry tickets online the day before; walk-ups are turned away after 9:30am most mornings. The Berber Museum inside the studio is included in the ticket and its collection of southern Moroccan jewelry, textiles, and musical instruments is genuinely excellent.

afternoon.notes: Opened in 2017 in a building by Studio KO - terracotta brise-soleil screens inspired by Moroccan latticework on a rigorous contemporary frame. The permanent gallery traces Saint Laurent's relationship with Morocco from his first visit in 1966 through his career, with rotating couture displays and audiovisual rooms. The temporary exhibition program is consistently strong. The building's interior reading room, with its collection of fashion and art books, is open to visitors and is one of the quietest rooms in Marrakech.

evening.activity_title: Menara Gardens and pavilion at sunset
evening.notes: A 12th-century Almohad olive grove of roughly 100 hectares surrounding a large basin fed by an underground khettara irrigation channel from the Atlas Mountains. The green-tiled Saadian pavilion reflects in the still water with the Atlas peaks behind it on clear afternoons - this is the postcard image of Marrakech that is not Jemaa el-Fna. Arrive an hour before sunset when the light catches the mountains directly. The garden itself is free; the pavilion interior costs a small fee and the interior view is unremarkable compared to the exterior reflection.

if_time_permits.notes: The 16th-century Saadian palace, stripped bare by Moulay Ismail who spent 12 years dismantling it to furnish his new capital at Meknes. What remains is a vast sunken courtyard, pools, and the stork-nested walls of what was once described by a Portuguese ambassador as one of the finest palaces in the world. The scale is still readable - walk the perimeter ramparts for the best sense of the original proportions. A minbar from the Koutoubia Mosque is displayed in a pavilion here, preserved since the 12th century.
